title: Error recovery with forced abort handles undefined state explicitly
description:
lora_l2.c:428-443 handles the case where the RX thread does not exit gracefully:

```c
LOG_WRN("RX thread join timed out after %dms, aborting thread. "
        "Any in-flight packet processing was terminated.",
        RX_THREAD_QUICK_JOIN_MS + RX_TIMEOUT_MS);
k_thread_abort(&rx_thread_data);
k_thread_join(&rx_thread_data, K_FOREVER);
/*
 * Mark module as requiring re-initialization. k_thread_abort()
 * may have terminated the thread while it held lora_mutex or
 * while inside a callback. The callback may hold its own locks
 * or have allocated resources that are now leaked. Safe restart
 * requires full de-init/re-init cycle.
 */
atomic_set(&lora_state.aborted, 1);
LOG_WRN("Module requires lichen_lora_l2_deinit() before restart");
```

And lichen_lora_l2_deinit() (lines 450-538) includes an extensive comment explaining that mutex reinitialization after abort is undefined behavior, but proceeds anyway because:
1. Alternative (refusing to deinit) leaves module permanently unusable
2. In practice, aborts usually happen outside critical sections
3. The aborted flag forces full deinit/init cycle

This is honest engineering: acknowledging the undefined behavior, explaining why recovery is attempted anyway, and documenting the only truly safe recovery (system reboot).
close_reason: Positive finding - no action needed, documents good practice
